metadata.title: Correlated Failure in AI Auditing — Consensus Is Not Verification
metadata.topic: CONDUCTING AI — CORRELATED FAILURE IN AI AUDITING

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The point that LLM agreement is evidence of shared priors, not correctness, plus the three structural judge biases, positional, verbosity, and self-enhancement, made me want the full brief. Help me: (1) work through the Bishop 14.2 ensemble result on why cross-checking only reduces error under independent failure modes; (2) document each of the three LLM-as-judge biases with a concrete example of when it would fire; (3) build the audit-pairing table matching factual claims to retrieval, math to sympy, and schema to a JSON validator, each with a structurally independent failure mode.

props.command: The point that LLM agreement is evidence of shared priors, not correctness, plus the three structural judge biases, positional, verbosity, and self-enhancement, made me want the full brief. Help me: (1) work through the Bishop 14.2 ensemble result on why cross-checking only reduces error under independent failure modes; (2) document each of the three LLM-as-judge biases with a concrete example of when it would fire; (3) build the audit-pairing table matching factual claims to retrieval, math to sympy, and schema to a JSON validator, each with a structurally independent failure mode.
props.topic: CONDUCTING AI — CORRELATED FAILURE IN AI AUDITING

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

Ensemble theory — Bishop section 14.2 — shows that cross-checking reduces error only when the checkers fail independently. If two checkers share the same failure mode, their agreement tells you nothing about correctness. LLMs used to audit LLMs share training data, RLHF tuning, and distributional biases. When they agree, that is not evidence of correctness — it is evidence of shared priors. Three documented LLM-as-judge failures: positional bias (favoring the first answer), verbosity bias (favoring longer answers), and self-enhancement bias (favoring outputs that match its own style). These are not bugs; they are structural.
beats.visual_intent: title card: correlated failure — ensemble theory requires independent failure modes; LLM-on-LLM fails this requirement

beats.narration_text: Ask for the research brief. In the terminal: claude — research the correlated-failure problem in AI-on-AI auditing. Cover three things: the ensemble theory result on independent failure modes from Bishop section 14.2; three documented LLM-as-judge failures — positional bias, verbosity bias, and self-enhancement bias; and a practical audit-pairing table showing which tools have independent failure modes from LLMs — code, retrieval, schema validation, and human domain check.

props.command:
claude "Research the correlated-failure problem in AI-on-AI
auditing: (1) ensemble theory result — cross-checking reduces
error only when checkers fail independently (Bishop 14.2);
(2) three LLM-as-judge failures — positional bias, verbosity
bias, self-enhancement bias; (3) practical audit-pairing table:
which tools have independent failure modes from LLMs
(code, retrieval, schema, human domain check)."

beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. The research brief is a markdown document. The key table is the audit-pairing table: factual claim paired with LLM-on-LLM is correlated failure — pair it with retrieval lookup instead. Math result paired with LLM-on-LLM is correlated — pair with eval or sympy. Schema match paired with LLM-on-LLM is correlated — pair with a JSON validator. Each right-hand column has a structurally different failure mode from the LLM.

props.code:
# correlated_failure_brief.md — research brief with citations

## Ensemble Theory (Bishop §14.2)
Error reduction from cross-checking requires that checkers
fail INDEPENDENTLY. If P(both wrong) = P(A wrong)^2, ensemble
works. If P(both wrong) = P(A wrong), it does not.

LLMs share training data + RLHF → correlated failure modes.

## LLM-as-Judge Failures
1. Positional bias: favors the first answer presented
2. Verbosity bias: favors longer, more detailed answers
3. Self-enhancement: favors outputs matching its own style

## Audit Pairing Table
CLAIM TYPE      | WRONG PAIRING | RIGHT PAIRING
Factual claim   | LLM-on-LLM   | Retrieval lookup
Math result     | LLM-on-LLM   | eval() / sympy
Schema match    | LLM-on-LLM   | JSON validator

beats.narration_text: The visualization splits into two panels. Left: correlated failure. Two LLM circles overlap — the shared bias region in crimson. When both agree, that agreement lives in the overlap. It is consensus, not verification. Right: independent audit. An LLM claim rectangle feeds into a code-check rectangle through an arrow. The two shapes do not overlap. Their failure modes are structurally different. Below: the audit pairing table maps claim types to the right verification tool.
beats.visual_intent: Manim B04_CorrelatedFailure: Venn left (correlated) vs separate shapes right (independent), audit pairing mini-table below

beats.narration_text: The brief mentions positional bias but does not include a reproducible demonstration. Ask for a revision: add a minimal Python example that demonstrates positional bias by sending the same two answers to the API in both orders and comparing the verdicts.

props.command:
claude "update correlated_failure_brief.md:
  -> add minimal Python example demonstrating positional bias
  -> send same 2 answers to API in both orders
  -> compare verdicts and print the bias direction"

beats.role_note: slate — fill with updated brief + demo output
beats.narration_text: Re-run. The updated brief includes a twelve-line Python demonstration. Order A picks answer one; order B picks answer two. The model changed its verdict based on position, not content. That is the correlated failure made concrete.
beats.visual_intent: updated research brief with positional bias demo code and sample output showing order-dependent verdicts

beats.narration_text: More AI does not mean better verification. It means more consensus from systems that share failure modes. The ensemble theory requirement is independence. LLMs do not meet it when auditing each other. The right pairing is a tool with structurally different failure modes: code for math, retrieval for facts, schema validators for structure, human domain experts for edge.
beats.visual_intent: summary card: correlated vs independent — 'checkers must fail differently'; audit pairing table recap

beats.narration_text: Your move: look at your current AI pipeline and identify every place where an LLM output is being verified by another LLM call. That is a correlated seam. Pick the highest-stakes one and replace the LLM audit with a structurally independent check — eval, a retrieval call, a schema validator, or a named human reviewer.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) find correlated seams in your pipeline, 2) replace highest-stakes one with independent check

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. More AI does not mean better verification. It means more consensus from systems that share failure modes. The ensemble theory requirement is independence. LLMs do not meet it when auditing each other. The right pairing is a tool with structurally different failure modes: code for math, retrieval for facts, schema validators for structure, human domain experts for edge. Your move: look at your current AI pipeline and identify every place where an LLM output is being verified by another LLM call. That is a correlated seam. Pick the highest-stakes one and replace the LLM audit with a structurally independent check — eval, a retrieval call, a schema validator, or a named human reviewer.
